title: A Derry On A Cove
content:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Twas in the felonן¿½s dock he stood, his eyes were black and blue;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ His voice with grief was broken, and his nose was broken, too;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ He muttered, as that broken nose he wiped upon his cap,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Itן¿½s orfal when the pן¿½leece has got a derry on a chap.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½I am a honest workinן¿½ cove, as any bloke can see,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Itן¿½s just because the pן¿½leece has got a derry, sir, on me;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Oh, yes, the legal gents can grin, I say it ainן¿½t no joke,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Itן¿½s cruel when the pן¿½leece has got a derry on a bloke.ן¿½

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Why donן¿½t you go to work?ן¿½ he said (he muttered, ן¿½Why donן¿½t you?ן¿½).
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Yer honer knows as well as me there ainן¿½t no work to do.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½And when I try to find a job Iן¿½m shaddered by a trap,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Itן¿½s awful when the pן¿½leece has got a derry on a chap.ן¿½

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ I sighן¿½d and shed a tearlet for that noble nature marred,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ But, ah! the Bench was rough on him, and gave him six monthsן¿½ hard.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ He only said, ן¿½Beyond the grave youן¿½ll cop it hot, by Jove!
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½There ainן¿½t no angel pן¿½leece to get a derry on a cove.ן¿½
meaning:
This evocative piece by Henry Lawson represents a masterful exploration of classic. The lines capture a profound emotional resonance, inviting the reader to reflect on the deeper themes of human experience and artistic expression.

In the broader context of Henry Lawson's bibliography, these specific lines stand out for their clarity and poignancy. Whether you are searching for inspiration or a moment of quiet contemplation, this work offers a timeless perspective that transcends its original era.

At Linespedia, we have curated this specific poem to ensure it reaches a modern audience. We recommend sharing these lines as a digital poster or using them as a thoughtful caption to connect with others who appreciate the enduring beauty of verse.
